## Goal
Short-TTL Ed25519 capability tokens allow members to perform offline operations (work:close, work:claim, etc.) without a convention server round-trip.

## Done condition
- pkg/captoken/ exists: token format, Sign(), Verify() (~60 LOC)
- Token struct: {subject, campfire, role, operations[], issued_at, expires_at, binding_msg_id}
- Signed by convention server's private key (the one named in the server-binding)
- Verify checks: signature against bound server pubkey, expires_at > now, operation in token.operations, subject matches sender
- TTLs: bulk safe ops (work:create/claim/update) = 48h; consequential (work:close/delegate) = 24h; high-stakes (work:gate-resolve) = 4h
- Test: token signed by server key, verified offline against binding pubkey → accepted
- Test: expired token → rejected
- Test: wrong operation in token → rejected
- Test: mismatched subject → rejected

## Files (§10 Wave 2)
- New: pkg/captoken/ — ~60 LOC

## Design reference
Design campfire: 36284e31fe77a3ecea91162d7c8826adc74b7dd1296f89ae69db68fab48a71cc. See §3.5 Offline Authorization: Capability Tokens. Revocation during TTL is intentional trade-off: token-TTL latency is documented. Reconciliation on reconnect: server reads log, posts retroactive fulfills messages.

Project campfires use E2E encryption by default. Org observers read a shadow summary campfire maintained by the convention server, not the main campfire.

## Done condition

- Convention server: on every work:item-* operation, writes a work:item-summary to the bound summary campfire (title, status, priority, assignee, eta, updated_at — no body/comments)

## Goal
Three new commands handle the full invite workflow: join a project by name, admit a requester, revoke a member.

## Done condition
- rd join <name> resolves the name, attempts to join, posts work:join-request if consult policy bounces it, blocks/polls for admission grant
- rd admit <item-id> posts work:role-grant for the requester + triggers admission via convention server; work item closes on success
- rd admit <item-id> --role agent → grants agent role
- rd admit <item-id> --deny "reason" → denies the request (work item closes)
- rd revoke <pubkey-or-name> → posts work:role-grant role=revoked
- rd revoke <pubkey-or-name> --retroactive → posts retroactive revocation for every member that key admitted
- DoS defense: work:join-request rate-limited per source pubkey at convention server (10/h default, configurable)
- Test: full round-trip: rd join → rd admit → second user can run rd list on project

## Files (§10 Wave 3)
- cmd/rd/admit.go — new command (~80 LOC; avoid approve.go collision)
- cmd/rd/join.go — new command (~100 LOC)
- cmd/rd/revoke.go — new command (~40 LOC)

## Design reference
Design campfire: 36284e31fe77a3ecea91162d7c8826adc74b7dd1296f89ae69db68fab48a71cc. See §4.1 (consult join policy), §4.3 (rd admit), §4.5 (revocation).
IMPORTANT: rd admit calls TWO things atomically: (1) posts work:role-grant, (2) triggers convention server admission (which calls SDK join-grant). If the second step fails, the role-grant must be rolled back.

## Campfire protocol
- On start: cf <SWARM_CF> claim-item --item_id <this-item-id> --description "implementing Wave 3 rd join/admit/revoke commands"

## Goal
When a member's role is revoked, items they have claimed (status=in_progress, claimed by their pubkey) automatically become claimable again by other members.

## Done condition
- state.go Derive(): on work:role-grant role=revoked, all items with status=in_progress claimed by the revoked pubkey flip to status=ready
- Other members see these items in rd ready
- Test: member claims 3 items; their key is revoked; rd ready for other members shows those 3 items
- Test: retroactive revocation (--retroactive) propagates to all items claimed by any member admitted by the revoked key

## Files (§10 Wave 3)
- pkg/state/state.go — stranded-item reclaim rule on role=revoked (~30 LOC)

## Design reference
Design campfire: 36284e31fe77a3ecea91162d7c8826adc74b7dd1296f89ae69db68fab48a71cc. See §4.5 revocation and TOCTOU. The reclaim rule is a state-derivation rule — no new messages needed. Retroactive key revocation (--retroactive) posts a cascade of work:role-grant role=revoked messages for every member the revoked key admitted.

## test-depth: feature
## agent-type: implementer
## model-tier: haiku

## Goal
State derivation honors convention:server-binding for consequential operations, accepts all messages when no binding is present (bypass mode).

## Architecture: two-pass Derive()
The current Derive() is a single-pass message replay. Role authorization is Class 1 (deterministic from messages). Implement as a two-pass approach:

Pass 1 — build role map: scan all messages once for work:role-grant tags. Build map[senderPubkey]roleInfo. Latest role-grant for a pubkey wins. role='revoked' removes from map.

Pass 2 — existing replay: process operations as before, but add fulfillment gating for consequential ops (work:close, work:delegate, work:gate-resolve):
  - Find the active server-binding message (convention:server-binding tag, most recent valid_from <= now)
  - If no binding exists in the log → bypass mode: accept all (Wave 1 behavior)
  - If binding exists: look for a 'fulfills' message from the bound server_pubkey, OR a valid capability token in the operation payload
  - Pre-binding items (timestamp before first binding valid_from) → implicitly authorized
  - If neither fulfillment nor token nor bypass → operation rejected (dropped from derived state)

DO NOT add a projection store parameter to Derive(). Build the role map from messages inline. This preserves the pure function contract and is consistent with the append-only replay model.

## Done condition
- Test: operation with valid server fulfillment → accepted
- Test: operation with valid capability token → accepted offline
- Test: operation with no fulfillment + no token + no binding → accepted (bypass mode)
- Test: operation with no fulfillment + no token + binding present → rejected
- Test: pre-binding item (no binding at timestamp) → accepted
- All tests use real campfire message slices — no mocked state.

## Files (§10 Wave 2)
- pkg/state/state.go:245 Derive() — ~80-120 LOC added to the existing function
- Read pkg/state/state.go fully before modifying — understand existing msg loop structure (switch on hasTag)

## Design reference
Design campfire: 36284e31fe77a3ecea91162d7c8826adc74b7dd1296f89ae69db68fab48a71cc. See §3.1 server-binding, §3.2 bypass mode, §3.5 capability tokens, §3.4 member-roles (Class 1 = derivable from log directly).
Bypass mode is MANDATORY — do not merge without it.

## test-depth: feature
## agent-type: implementer
## model-tier: sonnet

## Goal
When a user runs rd join and the campfire's consult policy fires, the convention server materializes a work:join-request item visible in the maintainer's rd ready queue.

## Architecture: how SDK consult policy works
The consult join policy is in pkg/naming/join_policy.go. When a campfire has Policy='consult', the SDK posts the join request as a message to the ConsultCampfire (a second campfire ID). The convention server must:
1. Create or use a dedicated 'maintainer inbox' campfire as the ConsultCampfire
2. Write join-policy.json (at cfHome/join-policy.json) pointing ConsultCampfire to that inbox campfire ID
3. Join the inbox campfire as a member
4. Watch the inbox campfire for messages tagged with the join-request convention
5. For each join-request message, materialize a work:join-request item in the project campfire

The convention server does NOT 'run' the callback as a function. The SDK posts to the ConsultCampfire ID; the convention server watches that campfire. This is campfire-to-campfire messaging, not a function hook.

## test-depth: feature
## agent-type: implementer
## model-tier: sonnet

## Goal
A goroutine-based in-process convention server inside rd handles the solo authorization path: issues fulfillments, posts a server-binding on first use, issues capability tokens.

## How convention.Server works (read first)
pkg/convention/server.go — convention.NewServer(client, decl) creates a server for one declaration. RegisterHandler(opName, fn) registers a handler per operation. Serve(ctx, campfireID) blocks, polling for messages. Run one goroutine per operation you want to handle.

The server enforces min_operator_level automatically when WithProvenance is set. For the in-process solo server, use a ProvenanceChecker that always returns level 1 (self-asserted) since the user is local.

## Authorization matrix (§3.6) — embed this in the server
| Operation          | min_operator_level |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| work:create        | 1                 |
| work:claim         | 1                 |
| work:update        | 1                 |
| work:close         | 2                 |
| work:delegate      | 2                 |
| work:gate-resolve  | 2                 |
| work:role-grant    | 2                 |

Level 2 operations require a fulfillment from the convention server itself. The in-process server handles this by: receiving the operation, authorizing it (role check + level check), then posting a fulfills message back to the campfire.

## Done condition
- pkg/conventionserver/ package exists: authorization matrix, capability token issuance, server-binding bootstrap (~200 LOC)
- Server starts as a goroutine; posts convention:server-binding on first use (naming itself via its own pubkey)
- Server signs fulfillments with the rd process identity key (ephemeral — tied to rd process lifetime)
- member-roles named filter projection declared at server startup: predicate=(tag "work:role-grant"), entity_key=payload.pubkey, refresh=on-write — use store.UpsertProjectionEntry to maintain it on each work:role-grant message the server sees
- cmd/rd/root.go requireClient() boots the in-process server when no convention:server-binding exists yet in the campfire (first run) or when one exists naming this process's pubkey
- Test: rd close item-id in solo mode → fulfillment message appears in campfire log, signed by local identity key
- Test: server-binding message appears in campfire log on first close operation

payload.title: ready-865: state_fulfillment_test uses string literal pubkeys, not real Ed25519 keys

payload.context: In state_fulfillment_test.go (work/ready-865), the bound server pubkey is set to the string literal 'server-key-hex'. Derive() compares sender strings so the test passes, but no real Ed25519 key encoding is exercised. A bug in hex normalization or key format handling in the real implementation would not be caught. Fix: generate a real Ed25519 keypair in the test, use its hex-encoded public key as the server_pubkey and msg sender.

payload.title: ready-f5d: TestServerFulfillsCloseOperation does not verify fulfillment message signature

payload.context: In pkg/conventionserver/server_test.go (worktree agent-af27b126), TestServerFulfillsCloseOperation verifies that a fulfillment message appears with the correct antecedent ID, but does NOT verify the fulfillment message's cryptographic signature or that the sender matches the server's identity. A server that posts an unsigned fulfillment or one with a forged sender field would pass this test. Fix: after finding the fulfillment message, call protocol.Client.Verify() (or inspect msg.Signature) to assert the message was signed by the server's private key.
